title: AI can stay controllable, safe, and resistant to misuse
description: An AI system can follow explicit constraints, refuse harmful behavior, resist adversarial prompting, and remain steerable after change.

subQuestions.title: Can it follow explicit policies and constraints?
subQuestions.description: Measures whether the system adheres to stated rules and operating boundaries during task execution.
subQuestions.rationale: Constraint following is now strong on many standard safety and workflow policy settings, though edge cases still remain.

proofItems.title: Benchmark suite: Policy and constraint adherence
proofItems.shortExplanation: Safety and workflow benchmark suites show strong adherence to explicit rule sets in most ordinary task conditions.

subQuestions.title: Can it refuse harmful requests consistently?
subQuestions.description: Tests whether harmful instructions are declined robustly across paraphrases and repeated attempts.
subQuestions.rationale: Refusal quality is improved but still not stable enough under paraphrase pressure and determined probing.

proofItems.title: Red-team evaluation: Refusal stability under harmful request variation
proofItems.shortExplanation: Adversarial safety evaluations show solid ordinary refusal behavior, but still find inconsistency under paraphrase and persistence.

subQuestions.title: Can it offer safe alternatives where appropriate?
subQuestions.description: Measures whether the system gives constructive lower-risk pathways instead of only flat refusal when a legitimate safer route exists.
subQuestions.rationale: Constructive redirection exists in many domains, but quality varies substantially and is not yet consistently useful.

proofItems.title: Controlled study: Safe alternative generation after refusal
proofItems.shortExplanation: User studies show many better-than-baseline redirections, but still highlight inconsistency in how helpful those alternatives are.

subQuestions.title: Can it resist jailbreaks and adversarial prompting?
subQuestions.description: Tests whether safety behavior survives intentionally hostile prompting and prompt-wrapper attacks.
subQuestions.rationale: Adversarial prompting remains one of the clearest unresolved gaps between acceptable lab behavior and robust real deployment.

proofItems.title: Red-team evaluation: Jailbreak resistance under adversarial prompting
proofItems.shortExplanation: Red-team tracks continue to expose meaningful safety degradation under sophisticated adversarial prompt attacks.

subQuestions.title: Can it remain steerable after prompt or capability changes?
subQuestions.description: Measures whether updates, wrappers, or changing task contexts preserve controllability instead of eroding it unpredictably.
subQuestions.rationale: Steerability persists in many ordinary settings, but regressions still appear after prompt changes, tool additions, and model updates.

proofItems.title: Deployment audit: Steerability retention after system changes
proofItems.shortExplanation: Release and deployment audits show that control quality can shift after prompt or capability changes, even when headline behavior looks stable.
